findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, extra-axial cystic lesion located in the posterior fossa that perfectly follows cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) attenuation on CT and signal intensity on all MRI sequences. It demonstrates smooth margins and exerts local mass effect on the adjacent cerebellar hemisphere.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the absence of internal vascular flow voids and normal appearances of the cerebellar vermis. Additionally, there may be associated smooth scalloping or remodeling of the adjacent inner table of the occipital bone due to long-standing CSF pulsations.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of restricted diffusion to suggest an epidermoid cyst, nor is there cystic dilatation of the fourth ventricle. Furthermore, there is no internal or peripheral contrast enhancement to suggest a cystic neoplasm.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a non-enhancing extra-axial fluid collection with identical signal to CSF that exerts definite local mass effect.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Arachnoid cyst, given the extra-axial location, true CSF signal characteristics including complete FLAIR suppression, and definite mass effect causing local cerebellar compression. These benign cysts do not communicate freely with the subarachnoid space and typically remain stable in size.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Mega cisterna magna, however this normal variant does not exert mass effect on the cerebellum or remodel the adjacent calvarium. Furthermore, a mega cisterna magna freely communicates with the subarachnoid space and frequently contains internal vascular flow voids and dural folds, which are absent here.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Dandy–Walker malformation, given that this structural anomaly presents with a large posterior fossa cyst, and missing it would fail to address the underlying vermian aplasia and associated supratentorial anomalies. However, in this case, the cerebellar vermis is fully formed and the fourth ventricle is morphologically normal.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to confirm the simple cystic nature of the lesion and assess for any secondary complications such as obstructive hydrocephalus due to mass effect on the fourth ventricle.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is helpful to confirm the congenital and typically stable nature of the cyst, or to detect gradual enlargement which may prompt surgical fenestration.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain if only an initial CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for definitively characterising the fluid as identical to CSF, assessing for subtle vermian anomalies, and confidently excluding an epidermoid cyst.
management.mriSequences: I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confirm the absence of restricted diffusion, reliably differentiating it from an epidermoid cyst, alongside FLAIR to confirm complete signal suppression matching CSF.
management.followUp: If the cyst is small and the patient is entirely asymptomatic, no specific imaging follow-up is generally required.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurosurgery MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of the cyst, the degree of mass effect on the cerebellum and brainstem, and the ventricular size to guide whether conservative management or surgical fenestration is indicated.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, extra-axial cystic lesion located in the posterior fossa that perfectly follows cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) attenuation on CT and signal intensity on all MRI sequences. It demonstrates smooth margins and exerts local mass effect on the adjacent cerebellar hemisphere. Supporting features include the absence of internal vascular flow voids and normal appearances of the cerebellar vermis. Additionally, there may be associated smooth scalloping or remodeling of the adjacent inner table of the occipital bone due to long-standing CSF pulsations. Importantly, there is no evidence of restricted diffusion to suggest an epidermoid cyst, nor is there cystic dilatation of the fourth ventricle. Furthermore, there is no internal or peripheral contrast enhancement to suggest a cystic neoplasm. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a non-enhancing extra-axial fluid collection with identical signal to CSF that exerts definite local mass effect.

The appearances are most in keeping with Arachnoid cyst, given the extra-axial location, true CSF signal characteristics including complete FLAIR suppression, and definite mass effect causing local cerebellar compression. These benign cysts do not communicate freely with the subarachnoid space and typically remain stable in size. The principle differential would be Mega cisterna magna, however this normal variant does not exert mass effect on the cerebellum or remodel the adjacent calvarium. Furthermore, a mega cisterna magna freely communicates with the subarachnoid space and frequently contains internal vascular flow voids and dural folds, which are absent here. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Dandy–Walker malformation, given that this structural anomaly presents with a large posterior fossa cyst, and missing it would fail to address the underlying vermian aplasia and associated supratentorial anomalies. However, in this case, the cerebellar vermis is fully formed and the fourth ventricle is morphologically normal.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to confirm the simple cystic nature of the lesion and assess for any secondary complications such as obstructive hydrocephalus due to mass effect on the fourth ventricle. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is helpful to confirm the congenital and typically stable nature of the cyst, or to detect gradual enlargement which may prompt surgical fenestration. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain if only an initial CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for definitively characterising the fluid as identical to CSF, assessing for subtle vermian anomalies, and confidently excluding an epidermoid cyst. I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confirm the absence of restricted diffusion, reliably differentiating it from an epidermoid cyst, alongside FLAIR to confirm complete signal suppression matching CSF. If the cyst is small and the patient is entirely asymptomatic, no specific imaging follow-up is generally required. This should go to the Neurosurgery MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of the cyst, the degree of mass effect on the cerebellum and brainstem, and the ventricular size to guide whether conservative management or surgical fenestration is indicated.
